data.title: Lennox raises dividend by 13%
data.description:
• Lennox (NYSE:LII) declared $1.30/share quarterly dividend, 13% increase from prior dividend of $1.15.
  • Forward yield 0.91%
  • Payable July 15; for shareholders of record June 30; ex-div June 30.
  • See LII Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: CSW Industrials outlines fiscal 2026 revenue and EBITDA growth targets with new Aspen acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSWI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Armes, Chairman, CEO and President, announced "record results for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted earnings per diluted share, and adjusted net income for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025." He highlighted fiscal fourth quarter revenue of $231 million, adjusted EBITDA of $60 million, adjusted earnings per diluted share of $2.24, and adjusted net income of $38 million. Armes also revealed "full-year revenue of $878 million, adjusted EBITDA of $228 million, including margin expansion of 70 basis points to 25.9%...adjusted earnings per diluted share of $8.41, adjusted net income of $137 million and cash flow from operations of $168 million."
  • Armes announced the acquisition of Aspen Manufacturing for $313.5 million, stating that "Aspen will expand our HVAC/R product offering with the addition of market leading evaporator coils and air handlers." The acquisition is described as "the second largest acquisition our company has made."
  • Armes shared, "We announced in late April, that the company will be moving to the New York Stock Exchange on June 9th. We believe this strategic move...will be beneficial to all shareholders and provide additional liquidity."
  • James Perry, EVP and CFO, reported, "We delivered record revenue of $878 million, representing growth of 11%. $38 million of the growth was organic with the remaining $48 million of growth coming from the acquisitions of Dust Free, PSP Products and PF Waterworks, that we completed since February 2024."
  • Perry noted, "Operating leverage on this revenue drove 14% growth in adjusted EBITDA, along with 70 basis points of margin expansion and over 20% growth in adjusted earnings per diluted share."

Outlook

  • Perry stated, "As we look into fiscal 2026, we anticipate delivering full-year growth in revenue and adjusted EBITDA for each segment as well as consolidated EPS growth and even stronger growth in operating cash flow than in fiscal year 2025."
  • Management expects Aspen's fiscal 2026 revenue "to grow in the high single to low-double-digits off their trailing 12-month revenue of $125 million" and Aspen's EBITDA margin "to be approximately 24% for the full fiscal year 2026."
  • "We expect Contractor Solution's overall adjusted EBITDA margin for the full fiscal year 2026 to be in the low 30s versus the recent margins closer to the mid-30s as we layer in our acquisitions and the expected impact of tariffs," Perry explained.
  • The company expects "higher full-year EBITDA margin on higher revenue" for both Specialized Reliability Solutions and Engineered Building Solutions in 2026.
  • Management stated, "We expect to see EPS growth in fiscal 2026, although the company does not anticipate EPS to grow as a percentage as much as revenue and EBITDA due to the additional shares outstanding...increased interest expense and the increased intangible amortization from recent acquisitions."

Financial Results

  • Perry reported, "Consolidated gross profit in the fiscal fourth quarter was $102 million, representing 9% growth over the prior year period. Our gross profit margin remained relatively flat at 44.2%, compared to 44.4% in the prior year period."
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $60 million with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.9%.
  • Contractor Solutions accounted for $166 million in revenue and delivered $24.7 million or 17.5% growth versus the prior year quarter. Segment adjusted EBITDA was $56 million or 33.7% of revenue.
  • Specialized Reliability Solutions segment revenue decreased by 9% to $38 million, with segment EBITDA of $5.8 million and margin contraction to 15.3%.
  • Engineered Building Solutions segment revenue decreased by 4% to $28.7 million, with segment EBITDA of $4.2 million and margin of 14.5%.
  • Cash flow from operations for Q4 was $27 million, up 22% compared to the prior year. Free cash flow for the quarter was $22.8 million.
  • Perry highlighted that "we used most of our cash on hand at that time and borrowed $135 million from our revolving credit facility to fund the Aspen acquisition on May 1st."
  • The company renewed, extended, and upsized its revolving credit facility to $700 million.

Q&A

  • Jon Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Asked about the impact of tariffs on COGS, especially regarding Vietnam. Perry responded that "Tariffs is obviously very dynamic...there's still a 10% tariff everywhere and 30% plus in China...We expect for fiscal '26 for that number to be at/or a little less than 10%." Aspen's US-based operations are expected to mitigate some of this exposure.
  • Tanwanteng questioned M&A pipeline and revolver usage. Armes indicated a "very disciplined approach" to acquisitions, with the revolver available for larger deals. He noted a "robust and strong" target list for acquisitions but would likely wait for international manufacturing uncertainty to settle before further action.
  • Tanwanteng asked about Aspen's margin accretion. Perry said, "From an EBITDA accretion perspective, it's accretive day one from a dollar perspective, but the margins on consolidated, we think we will get there in the kind of near to mid-term."
  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs: Asked about tariff exposure as a percent of COGS. Perry clarified, "That's on COGS and that is specific to Contractor Solutions."
  • Maklari inquired about pricing actions and margin implications. Perry explained, "The price increase...will take place likely during this quarter...we think we're matching that pretty well with pricing as we need to."
  • Maklari asked about inventory and demand for the busy spring and summer season. Perry responded that "things feel relatively normal" and noted that pull forward on equipment due to regulation changes "just hasn't affected us a whole lot."
  • Sam Reed, Wells Fargo: Asked about Aspen margin guidance and swings. Perry said Aspen's margins could fluctuate "several hundred basis-points either side of that 24%."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities: Asked about demand trends into April/May. Perry stated that Specialized Reliability Solutions had a soft fourth quarter but improved as they moved into the new quarter. Contractor Solutions organic growth is expected to remain healthy with "mid to high single digit growth" over the long term.
  • Natalia Bak, Citi: Asked about timing to achieve 20% EBITDA margin for Engineered Building Solutions. Perry said, "20% is still the intermediate term hurdle...Are we there for the full year fiscal '26? Probably a little early to say that necessarily, but we're approaching it."
  • Bak asked about Specialized Reliability Solutions margin compression and strategies for improvement. Perry cited lower volumes as the main factor and pointed to initiatives like relocating operations to Texas for cost savings and product development.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on potential margin compression from tariffs, integration of acquisitions, and expectations for organic growth, often asking detailed follow-up questions. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, with consistent congratulations for the quarter but persistent probing for clarity on risk factors and execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing "record results," "strong organic volume growth," and "disciplined" capital allocation. Perry’s responses conveyed confidence in pricing power and mitigation of tariff impacts, and Armes reiterated a positive long-term outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on freight, tariffs, and acquisition integration, but there was an increase in questions about margin sustainability and the incremental impact of the Aspen acquisition. Management’s tone remained upbeat, with slightly increased caution about margin compression due to tariffs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced the Aspen Manufacturing acquisition and the upcoming move to the NYSE, both new developments compared to the previous quarter.
  • Margin expansion in adjusted EBITDA and revenue growth continued, but management flagged expected margin compression in Contractor Solutions for fiscal 2026 due to tariffs and integration of acquisitions.
  • Analysts shifted their focus from freight and inventory management in Q3 to tariffs, pricing actions, and acquisition integration in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence remained high, but language around tariffs and margin impacts became more cautious.
  • Specialized Reliability Solutions and Engineered Building Solutions segments faced revenue and margin headwinds in Q4, whereas both segments posted growth in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted potential margin compression in Contractor Solutions from tariffs and acquisitions, indicating, "We are anticipating an overall cost of goods sold impact from increased tariffs."
  • Perry stated, "We continue to make strategic changes to our global supply chain to minimize the impact of tariffs and other potential disruptions."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing and effectiveness of pricing actions to offset tariff impacts and the pace of margin recovery for acquired businesses.

Final Takeaway

CSW Industrials closed fiscal 2025 with record revenue and earnings, supported by organic growth and recent acquisitions. The Aspen Manufacturing acquisition was completed and is expected to contribute high single to low-double-digit revenue growth and a 24% EBITDA margin in fiscal 2026. Management projects company-wide revenue and EBITDA growth in the coming year but anticipates some margin compression in Contractor Solutions from tariffs and integration-related costs, with mitigation strategies centered on pricing actions and supply chain adjustments. The move to the NYSE and expanded credit facilities position the company for future acquisitions and growth opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street seesaws as U.S. bond sell-off eases up
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages meandered on either side of the flat line on Thursday, while a bruising bond sell-off from the previous session eased up. The focus remained on President Trump's "One Big Beautiful" tax bill, which was narrowly passed by the U.S. House.

Stock index futures showed little reaction to the favorable vote, and the major averages eventually opened mixed. Moves have remained small since. The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last -0.03% at 5,842.59 points in afternoon trade, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.04% at 41,845.15 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.38% at 18,944.83 points.

U.S. Treasury yields had stabilized on Thursday after soaring on Wednesday, as traders had dumped bonds amid concerns over the new tax bill's effects on the ballooning U.S. fiscal deficit. The 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 4 basis points to 4.56%, while the shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 3 basis points to 4.01%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.  

Following days of marathon meetings and intense negotiations, including Republican efforts that lasted through the night, lawmakers early this morning voted 215-214 in favor of the bill that contains more than 1,000 pages and proposes about $3.8T in tax cuts, including the extension of those enacted by U.S. President Trump's Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) in 2017. 

But worries over the implications of the bill on the U.S. fiscal deficit have been on top of investors' minds.

"The die was cast here the moment both presidential tickets agreed to extend TCJA. That keeps revenues at 17% GDP as spending continues inexorable rise & deficits in 6-7% range. The issue is that GOP has taken off the table vast majority of spending progs. (Democrats) have no plan either," Donald Schneider, deputy head of U.S. policy at Piper Sandler, said on X (formerly Twitter) on Wednesday.

"GOP is doing something quite difficult now which is enacting real entitlement cuts and real tax increases. So they are demonstrating credibility that it can be done. But no one should be surprised that deficits will linger in 6%+ territory," Schneider said. 

"Fiscal outlook is basically set at this point. Resolution of the crisis will be left to next President in 2029. Unless both parties do total 180 on entitlement reform," he added. 

Though the tax bill dominated headlines on Thursday, there was also some economic data to focus on. The number of Americans filing for initial jobless claims in the past week slipped. Meanwhile, S&P Global's flash survey for May showed U.S. business activity growth and expectations for future output improving from April lows. Additionally, U.S. existing home sales unexpectedly dipped in April. 

Turning to active movers, solar stocks were crushed as the House passage of the tax bill hastens a potential end to key clean energy credits. Enphase Energy (ENPH) slumped -19.3% and was the top percentage loser on the S&P 500 (SP500).

data.title: Dorian LPG signals sustained market strength with 79% of Q1 days fixed at $42,000 TCE amid shifting trade dynamics
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dorian LPG Ltd. (LPG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Hadjipateras, Chairman, President, and CEO, highlighted a dividend of $0.50 per share, totaling $21.3 million, reflecting a continued commitment to "returning capital to shareholders in a manner that's aligned with market conditions and our policy of distributing earnings prudently." He noted that over $155 million in dividends were paid in the past fiscal year.
  • Hadjipateras emphasized that "increased production in the U.S. and the Middle East and U.S. terminal expansion, combined with just nine new buildings for the rest of the year, will support a balanced freight market and healthy earnings for 2025." He also expressed confidence in "the long-term fundamentals of LPG demand, which are underpinned by growing petrochemical and residential consumption, particularly in Asia and by infrastructure expansions in the U.S."
  • Operationally, Hadjipateras reported progress on investments in energy-saving devices and performance optimization, with 8 dry dockings planned for the year and ongoing vessel conversions to facilitate ammonia carriage.
  • Ted Young, CFO, stated, "At March 31, 2025, we reported $317 million free cash, which was sequentially up from the previous quarter. Cash flow from operations more than doubled from $24 million to $50.3 million quarter-over-quarter, and we generated $10 million from the maturity of some bond holdings, all of which gave us enough cash flow to support our dividend a progress payment on our new building made in January and our quarterly debt amortization."
  • Young cited a debt balance at quarter end of $557.4 million, noting a debt to total book capitalization of 34.8% and net debt to total capitalization at 15%, with an undrawn $50 million revolver and one debt-free vessel.

Outlook

  • Dorian LPG management projected that "our cash cost per day for the coming year to be approximately $26,000 per day, excluding capital expenditures for dry docking and progress payments on our new building."
  • For the quarter ending June 30, 2025, the company estimates that "we have fixed 79% of the pool’s available days in the quarter at a TCE of roughly $42,000 per day."
  • Management expects to drydock 8 vessels in the fiscal year ending March 2026, with a budgeted $12 million, excluding off-hire time.
  • The company continues to monitor trade tensions but maintains a "constructive market view" for 2025 on the back of terminal expansions and limited newbuild deliveries.

Financial Results

  • Free cash at quarter end was reported as $317 million, up from the previous quarter.
  • Cash flow from operations rose from $24 million to $50.3 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • The Helios Pool earned a TCE per day for its spot and COA voyage of $29,800 for the March 31 quarter, with Dorian's reported TCE revenue per available day at about $35,300.
  • Daily OpEx for the quarter was $11,000, excluding dry-docking related expenses, which was up from the prior quarter. Noncapitalized dry-docking expenses totaled about $3.2 million.
  • Reported adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $36.6 million.
  • The declared dividend of $0.50 per share reflects a reduction from the prior quarter, explained as “a balanced mix between results and the long-term needs and prospects of the business."

Q&A

  • Omar Nokta, Jefferies: "Could you maybe... talk about what's driving this market strength here recently? And is this sort of... are there any kind of trade pattern changes that have evolved out of what happened in April between China and the U.S.?"Tim Hansen: "We see that tightening that we still see these trade flows had that altered when the high tariff was in place, and China put on the statutory tariffs. So, we're still seeing a lot of cargoes going from the U.S. to India and to Southeast Asia. So that, of course, gives a lot of ton miles...we did have kind of a firm outlook for '25 already. And yes, with the change in trade routes, that has definitely helped, especially as we haven't seen any destruction of demand here in China."
  • Nokta followed up on the impact of the U.S.-China trade deal on trade lanes.Hansen: "It seems to have kind of settled now...as long as it's on balancess, you could say, it was immaterial, whether you go one or the other places. You will probably still try to place U.S. tonnes outside China just to make sure that no surprises bring upon you."
  • Nokta inquired about the dividend decision timing relative to China-U.S. trade talks.Ted Young: "The Board made the decision with the best information available at the time. And how the Board at its discretion decides to evaluate the environment when the next meets, we'll see."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a balanced but probing tone, focusing on the sustainability of strong spot rates, the impact of trade route changes, and the flexibility of dividend policy, reflecting cautious optimism amid global trade uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone in their prepared remarks, frequently emphasizing fundamentals, operational discipline, and market resilience. In the Q&A, management was factual and guarded, acknowledging uncertainty but reiterating a "constructive market view."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions were more focused on trade dynamics and market volatility, while management’s tone shifted from strategic caution to more operational detail and market adaptation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a dividend reduction from $0.70 to $0.50 per share, attributed to balancing results with long-term business needs and increased dry dockings.
  • Chartering results showed a marginal decline in TCE revenue per available day compared to the previous quarter, but forward bookings for the next quarter are projected to be stronger.
  • Cash flow from operations more than doubled quarter-over-quarter, and free cash increased slightly, indicating improved operational performance.
  • Analyst focus shifted from capital allocation and supply growth concerns in Q3 to freight market volatility and trade pattern impacts in Q4, with more attention on immediate earnings sustainability and dividend flexibility.
  • Management’s confidence remained steady but was more focused on near-term operational resilience and adaptability to external shocks like tariffs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited recent "rate gyrations" and trade policy volatility, particularly U.S.-China tariffs, as key variables affecting the business.
  • Hadjipateras acknowledged that "weather, terminaling fees, global petrochemical demand and global trade policies" are among the factors influencing results.
  • Increased drydocking and related expenses are anticipated, which could impact operational availability.
  • The company continues to monitor potential demand destruction if Chinese PDH demand weakens post-winter, balanced against ton-mile demand growth elsewhere.

Final Takeaway

Dorian LPG’s management reiterates confidence in the underlying fundamentals of the LPG shipping market, emphasizing prudent capital allocation, operational flexibility, and a constructive outlook for 2025 amid shifting trade patterns and tariff uncertainties. With 79% of next quarter’s available days fixed at a strong TCE rate and ongoing investments in fleet optimization, the company remains focused on balancing shareholder returns with long-term business sustainability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Société Générale: Dollar decline could boost Asian equities
data.description:
Société Générale analysts have identified a potential peak in the U.S. dollar (DXY) that could support broader investment into emerging Asian equities (NASDAQ:EEMA), (NASDAQ:AAXJ).

“Cracks in U.S. exceptionalism” combined with shifting asset valuations are creating favorable conditions for investors to consider rotating out of U.S. assets into emerging markets, said Frank Benzimra, head of Asia Equity Strategy.

Historical market trends show developed markets (VEA), (NYSEARCA:IDEV) outperformed Asia ex-Japan (NASDAQ:AAXJ), (NYSEARCA:FLAX) significantly over the past 30 years during periods of dollar strength, according to the firm’s research.

However, this pattern has reversed since President Trump’s inauguration, with the strategists observing that Asia ex-Japan outperforming “has coincided with a decline in the dollar index.”

Foreign investors are now returning to Asian equities after a period of outflows, according to Société Générale’s analysis. The research indicates this shift gained momentum particularly after the announcement of a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs around April 9, marking a significant turning point in investment flows.

The bank’s 30-year analysis reveals a positive correlation between Asian equity returns and local currency performance. Markets in Korea (EWY) and Taiwan (EWT) exhibit the highest such correlation, suggesting these regions may benefit significantly from current currency trends.

Consumer-related industries stand to gain the most from currency appreciation, Benzimra added. He highlighted that Japanese domestic demand stocks (NYSEARCA:EWJ) are particularly well-positioned to benefit from a stronger yen (NYSEARCA:FXY), as this “supports earnings and consumer purchasing power.”

Also, recent policy support in China and potential currency appreciation across the region create a supportive backdrop for Asian equities overall, according Benzimra.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump on Fannie privatization, Dimon talks stagflation
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of remarks and statements by newsmakers that could potentially move markets, sectors or individual stocks.

-President Trump said in a post on Truth Social Wednesday evening that he was  "giving very serious consideration to bringing Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) public" and "will be making a decision in the near future."

"Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) are doing very well, throwing off a lot of CASH, and the time would seem to be right. Stay tuned!" Trump added in his post.

- JP Morgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon said at the bank's Global China Summit in Shanghai on Thursday that there was a "chance" the U.S. could see stagflation and that "we have to be prepared for something like that." 

“Global fiscal deficits are inflationary. I think the remilitarization of the world is inflationary. The restructuring of trade is inflationary. And this is not all an American thing,” Dimon said during an interview in Shanghai, according to CNN.

-Wells Fargo (WF) said in an analysis report that the Trump administration's goal of re-shoring manufacturing jobs through raising tariffs isn't feasible due to higher U.S. labor costs. 

“A meaningful increase in factory jobs does not appear likely in the foreseeable future, in our view,” the report said, according to CNBC. “Higher prices and policy uncertainty may weigh on firms’ ability and willingness to expand payrolls.”

- Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said foreigners will be able to register for the Trump administration's new $5M "golden visa" program within the next several days.

“I expect there will be a website up called ‘Trump card dot gov’ in about a week,” Lutnick said at an Axios event in Washington, D.C., on Wednesday, according to CNN. “The details of that will come soon after, but people can start to register.”

data.title: Families of 737 Max crash victims urge DOJ to reject deal with Boeing: Bloomberg
data.description:
The families of victims killed in the two fatal Boeing (NYSE:BA) 737 Max crashes are pressing the U.S. Justice Department to pursue a criminal trial against the aerospace giant rather than finalize a proposed agreement that would shield it from prosecution, Bloomberg News reported Thursday.

In a letter sent Thursday, attorney Paul Cassell, representing the victims' families, described the reported nonprosecution agreement being considered as “deeply flawed” and warned it would amount to a “miscarriage of justice.” He urged the department to abandon settlement talks and move forward with the criminal case, which is scheduled for trial on June 23 in a Texas federal court.

“Rather than engage in further discussions about a pre-trial resolution, the Department should simply take the case to trial,” Cassell wrote, adding a request for a meeting with U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi to discuss the matter further.

Meeting with family members

According to court filings, federal prosecutors met with family members last Friday to outline their options, which include a potential plea deal, filing new charges such as criminal conspiracy, or pursuing the case at trial. A final decision has yet to be announced, and any resolution would require approval from U.S. District Judge Reed O’Connor, who is overseeing the proceedings.

Cassell said in his letter that the families will formally challenge any proposed nonprosecution agreement in court.

Victims’ families have long maintained that Boeing (NYSE:BA) and its executives should be held criminally accountable for design flaws tied to the 2018 and 2019 crashes that killed 346 people. To date, the only employee to face trial, a former manager responsible for pilot manuals, was acquitted.

Renewed scrutiny of safety

Back in 2021, Boeing struck a deal with the Justice Department to defer prosecution on a charge that it misled regulators about the MCAS system, which played a central role in both crashes. That agreement, along with Boeing’s (BA) guilty plea, was nearing expiration when a separate in-flight safety incident triggered renewed scrutiny into the company’s manufacturing standards.

In May of last year, federal prosecutors concluded Boeing (BA) had breached its earlier agreement and recommended criminal charges. The company reportedly agreed to plead guilty, pay penalties, and appoint an independent monitor as part of a plea arrangement. The proposed deal would have also included $455 million in safety and compliance investments.

However, Judge O’Connor rejected that plea deal, criticizing its limited oversight role for the court and objecting to provisions related to how the independent monitor would be selected—specifically language referencing diversity and inclusion criteria.

Since then, Boeing (BA) and prosecutors have continued discussions to reach a revised plea agreement. According to reports from the Wall Street Journal, Boeing (BA) attempted to withdraw its guilty plea in March. A day later, Judge O’Connor set the June trial date without elaborating on the decision.

The Justice Department has said it is still evaluating how to proceed, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: Fortescue delays timeline for Iron Bridge magnetite full capacity to 2028
data.description:
Miner Fortescue (OTCQX:FSUMF) said Thursday its Iron Bridge magnetite project in Western Australia likely will not reach nameplate capacity until FY 2028, nearly three years later than previously planned.

Fortescue (OTCQX:FSUMF) said it expects to ship 10M-12M metric tons/year at the site in its fiscal year through June 2026, rising to 16M-20M tons in H2 2027 before aiming to achieve nameplate capacity of 22M tons at the site in FY June 2028, after previously expecting to be operating at full capacity by September 2025.

Fortescue (OTCQX:FSUMF) has faced setbacks in developing the US$3.9B Iron Bridge project, including delays and cost overruns, but the company said the operation is on track to meet FY 2025 guidance for shipments and operating costs, after previously guiding for annual shipments of 5M-9M tons from the site this fiscal year.

Iron Bridge is a joint venture between Fortescue (OTCQX:FSUMF), which owns 69% of the operation, and Taiwan's Formosa Plastics, with magnetite shipment starting in 2023.

The project is key to Fortescue's (OTCQX:FSUMF) product diversification strategy, offering a high-grade concentrate that differs from its predominantly hematite output from the Pilbara region of Australia.

The company said it expects to ship a total of up to 200M tons of iron ore, mostly low-grade hematite from other pits it runs in the Pilbara.

data.title: SpaceX Starship Flight 9 cleared for launch
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration has cleared SpaceX (SPACE) to launch Starship Flight 9 following its investigation into the Starship Flight 8 explosion in March.

The agency determined that the company has satisfactorily addressed the causes of the mishap and will verify that SpaceX (SPACE) will implement all necessary corrective actions.

During the eighth test flight of Starship at SpaceX’s Starbase, Texas launch site, the upper stage experienced a rapid unscheduled disassembly during the ascent burn, resulting in an explosion. While the Super Heavy booster was successfully captured by the launch tower "Mechazilla," the Starship failed to complete its mission objectives, scattering debris over parts of Florida and the Caribbean. As a result, the FAA temporarily halted flights into Miami, Fort Lauderdale, Palm Beach, and Orlando airports.

The event required SpaceX (SPACE) to perform a “mishap investigation,” to determine the cause and identify corrective actions. The FAA must then determine that any system, process, or procedure related to the mishap does not affect public safety or any aspect of the operator’s license.  

SpaceX (SPACE) Starship Flight 9 is tentatively scheduled to launch on May 27 at 6:30pm CDT from Starbase, Texas. In response to the March accident, the FAA has nearly doubled the Aircraft Hazard Area (“AHA”) for Flight 9 to cover 1,600 nautical miles east from the Starbase launch site versus the AHA for Flight 8.

data.title: Prices of new U.S. drugs doubled over the past four years: report
data.description:
A new Reuters analysis has found that prices of newly launched medications in 2024 have more than doubled in the U.S. compared to 2021 as drug manufacturers have increasingly focused on developing rare disease therapies, which usually attract premium prices.

Citing a survey of 45 drugs, the publication noted that the median annual list price of a newly marketed treatment has exceeded $370K in 2024, over a two-fold rise from 2021, when the median price of 30 drugs launched through mid-July had approached $180K.

The findings come as markets witness a rising number of rare disease therapies, which are typically commercialized at high prices.

According to the IQVIA (IQV) Institute for Human Data Science, 51% of newly marketed drugs in 2019 targeted orphan diseases, a name reserved for conditions affecting less than 200K Americans. The figure has increased to 72% in 2024, with over 40% of orphan drug launches targeting cancer.

Orchard Therapeutics (ORTX), which was acquired by Japanese drugmaker Kyowa Kirin (OTCPK:KYKOF) (OTCPK:KYKOY) last year, has launched the most expensive medication in 2024, Reuters analysis suggested. The one-time gene therapy marketed as Lenmeldy for a rare inherited disorder called early-onset metachromatic leukodystrophy commanded a price tag of $4.25M.

Meanwhile, PTC Therapeutics (NASDAQ:PTC) came in second place with its gene therapy launched at a list price of $3.95M, beating the $3.5M price tag commanded by Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) for its hemophilia drug Beqves.

The New York-based pharma giant discontinued the one-time gene therapy developed with Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) early this year, citing concerns such as low patient uptake and a lack of interest among prescribers.

Treatments from listed drugmakers such as Mesoblast (MESO), Zevra Therapeutics (ZVRA), Adaptimmune Therapeutics (ADAP), and Merus (MRUS) were also part of the top ten list.
